_comment: One file per procedure_code. Distilled from the matching pair in PM JAY/Standard Treatment Guideline/master/<condition>/ : _Medco.pdf (clinical + mandatory docs) + _PPD.CPD.pdf (PPD/CPD processing checklists). Filename = procedure_code. This is what the Documentation Checker, Query Predictor, and Medical Justification agents read AFTER the picker has chosen the code.
clinical_indications: Bleeding per rectum – fresh blood in drops at the time of defecation (painless)
Prolapsing rectal mass / Protrusion from the anus
Confirmed presence of rectal polyp by digital rectal examination, colonoscopy, or rigid/flexible proctosigmoidoscopy

clinical_thresholds.note: confirmed by digital rectal examination, sigmoidoscopy, or colonoscopy

ppd_preauth.q: Clinical notes - detailed history, signs & symptoms, indication for procedure?
Digital rectal examination and Sigmoidoscopy/Colonoscopy for confirming polyp?

cpd_claim.q: Are the detailed ICPs with daily vitals and line of treatment?
Are the detailed procedure / Operative Notes available?
Is the Discharge summary with follow-up advise at the time of discharge?

common_queries: Presence of rectal polyp not confirmed by digital rectal examination, sigmoidoscopy, or colonoscopy
Clinical notes, detailed history, signs & symptoms, or indication for procedure are missing/incomplete at pre-authorization
Digital rectal examination or Sigmoidoscopy/Colonoscopy confirming polyp is missing/incomplete at pre-authorization
Detailed Indoor Case Papers with daily vitals and line of treatment are missing/incomplete at claim submission
Detailed procedure / Operative Notes are missing/incomplete at claim submission
Discharge summary with follow-up advice is missing/incomplete at claim submission
Histopathological examination report is missing/incomplete at claim submission

clinical_key_pointers: Polyps are abnormal growths rising from the lining of the large intestine. Polyps may be sessile (flat) or pedunculated (with a stalk) and vary considerably in size.
Juvenile polyps are the most common gastrointestinal (GI) polyps seen in children, common in age group of 3-6 years.
Juvenile polyps are lesions, which are typically solitary and commonly occur in the rectum or sigmoid colon.
Juvenile rectal polyps are isolated benign hamartomas and are a relatively common cause of rectal bleeding.
Rectal polyp is a benign adenomatous polyp, usually single.
surgical_options: Polypectomy (Sigmoidoscopy or Colonoscopy for isolated juvenile polyp)
additional_information.referral_criteria: If the polyps are more than five in number, periodic colonoscopic surveillance is mandatory
